content:
# Ephesians Chapter 1 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/EPH_001_page_001.jpg, EPH_001_page_002.jpg

1. Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus by the will of God, to the saints who are in Ephesus, and are faithful in Christ Jesus:

2. Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

3. Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us in Christ with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places,

4. even as he chose us in him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and blameless before him. In love

5. he predestined us for adoption as sons through Jesus Christ, according to the purpose of his will,

6. to the praise of his glorious grace, with which he has blessed us in the Beloved.

7. In him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses, according to the riches of his grace,

8. which he lavished upon us, in all wisdom and insight

9. making known to us the mystery of his will, according to his purpose, which he set forth in Christ

10. as a plan for the fullness of time, to unite all things in him, things in heaven and things on earth.

11. In him we have obtained an inheritance, having been predestined according to the purpose of him who works all things according to the counsel of his will,

12. so that we who were the first to hope in Christ might be to the praise of his glory.

13. In him you also, when you heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation, and believed in him, were sealed with the promised Holy Spirit,

14. who is the guarantee of our inheritance until we acquire possession of it, to the praise of his glory.

15. For this reason, because I have heard of your faith in the Lord Jesus and your love toward all the saints,

16. I do not cease to give thanks for you, remembering you in my prayers,

17. that the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of glory, may give you the Spirit of wisdom and of revelation in the knowledge of him,

18. having the eyes of your hearts enlightened, that you may know what is the hope to which he has called you, what are the riches of his glorious inheritance in the saints,

19. and what is the immeasurable greatness of his power toward us who believe, according to the working of his great might

20. that he worked in Christ when he raised him from the dead and seated him at his right hand in the heavenly places,

21. far above all rule and authority and power and dominion, and above every name that is named, not only in this age but also in the one to come.

22. And he put all things under his feet and gave him as head over all things to the church,

23. which is his body, the fullness of him who fills all in all.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Ephesians 1 contains two major sections: the great eulogy/berakah (vv.3-14) and Paul's prayer for the Ephesians (vv.15-23). All three translators correctly identified the triadic structure of the eulogy (Father/Son/Spirit) and the cosmic scope of the christology.

**Verses 3-14 — The eulogy:** All three translators handled this immense single sentence by breaking it into manageable English units while preserving the cascading logic. SOPHIA affirms: the tripartite structure with "to the praise of his glory" as refrain (vv.6, 12, 14) is the architectural key.

**Verse 10 — "To unite all things":** All three translators captured the cosmic scope of anakephalaiōsasthai. SOPHIA affirms: this is the thesis statement of the entire letter — cosmic reunification under Christ.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.1 — "to the holy ones who are in Ephesos" vs. "to the saints who are in Ephesus" vs. "to God's holy people in Ephesus"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to the holy [ones] who are in Ephesos and faithful in Christos Yeshua" | hagiois — "holy ones"; transliterated Ephesos |
| LOGOS | "to the saints who are in Ephesus, and are faithful in Christ Jesus" | "Saints" — standard |
| PNEUMA | "to God's holy people in Ephesus, who are faithful followers of Christ Jesus" | "God's holy people"; adds "followers" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Saints" is the standard rendering of hagioi. PNEUMA adds "God's" (implied but not stated) and "followers" (not in Greek — pistois means "faithful/believing," not "followers").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "God's" and "followers."

---

### v.3 — "in the heavenly places" vs. "in the heavenly realms"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in the heavenly [places]" | en tois epouraniois — "in the heavenlies" |
| LOGOS | "in the heavenly places" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "in the heavenly realms" | "Realms" — spatial expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. epouranios is an adjective used as a noun — "the heavenly [places/things]." "Places" is the standard English supply. PNEUMA's "realms" suggests sovereign territories — a valid interpretation but slightly more specific.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — "places" vs. "realms."

---

### v.4-5 — "in love" attachment: end of v.4 or beginning of v.5

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "before him in love, having predestined us" | en agapē attached to v.4 — "blameless before him in love" |
| LOGOS | "blameless before him. In love he predestined us" | en agapē attached to v.5 — "in love he predestined" |
| PNEUMA | "God loved us and chose us in Christ to be holy and without fault in his eyes. God decided in advance to adopt us" | Restructured; "loved us" from en agapē |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The placement of "in love" is a classic text-critical/syntactic question. Attaching to v.5 ("in love he predestined") gives the stronger theological statement — predestination is motivated by love. PNEUMA restructures entirely, making "loved us" a separate verb rather than a prepositional phrase.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. en agapē placement — does love modify our holiness (v.4) or God's predestination (v.5)? The answer shapes whether love describes the believer's character or God's motive.

---

### v.5 — "adoption as sons" vs. "adopt us into his own family"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "unto adoption as sons through Yeshua Christos to himself" | huiothesian — "adoption as sons" |
| LOGOS | "for adoption as sons through Jesus Christ" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "to adopt us into his own family by bringing us to himself through Jesus Christ" | "Into his own family"; adds "bringing us to himself" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. huiothesia is a technical Roman legal term — "adoption as sons" (the legal act conferring full inheritance rights). PNEUMA changes to "adopt us into his own family" — losing the legal precision and adding "bringing us to himself" (not in Greek).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA loses legal terminology and adds content.

---

### v.6 — "the glory of his grace" vs. "his glorious grace" vs. "the glorious grace he has poured out"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "unto [the] praise of [the] glory of his grace, with which he graced us in the Beloved" | doxēs tēs charitos — genitive chain; echaritōsen — cognate verb |
| LOGOS | "to the praise of his glorious grace, with which he has blessed us in the Beloved" | Compressed genitive; "blessed" for echaritōsen |
| PNEUMA | "So we praise God for the glorious grace he has poured out on us who belong to his dear Son" | "Poured out"; "dear Son" for "Beloved" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The cognate echaritōsen ("he graced") is difficult to render — LOGOS uses "blessed," which loses the cognate play but is clear. PNEUMA adds "poured out" (not the verb) and renders "the Beloved" (tō ēgapēmenō) as "his dear Son" — an interpretive identification.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA identifies "the Beloved" and adds "poured out."

---

### v.7 — "redemption" vs. "purchased our freedom"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we have the redemption through his blood" | apolytrōsin — "redemption/release by payment" |
| LOGOS | "we have redemption through his blood" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "he purchased our freedom with the blood of his Son" | "Purchased our freedom"; adds "his Son" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. apolytrōsis means "redemption/release" — a term from the slave market. PNEUMA's "purchased our freedom" captures one dimension but adds "his Son" (not in this verse — Paul says "his blood," not "the blood of his Son").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "his Son" and paraphrases.

---

### v.8 — "caused to abound" vs. "lavished" vs. "showered"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "which he caused to abound toward us" | eperisseusen — "made to overflow/abound" |
| LOGOS | "which he lavished upon us" | "Lavished" — standard |
| PNEUMA | "He has showered his kindness on us" | "Showered"; "kindness" for charis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. perisseuō means "to abound/overflow" — "lavished" captures the excess. PNEUMA changes "grace" to "kindness" and uses "showered" — both adequate but less precise.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — vocabulary choice.

---

### v.10 — "to sum up all things" vs. "to unite all things" vs. "bring everything together under the authority of Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to sum up all things in the Christos" | anakephalaiōsasthai — "recapitulate/bring under one head" |
| LOGOS | "to unite all things in him" | "Unite" — captures the result |
| PNEUMA | "he will bring everything together under the authority of Christ" | Adds "authority"; future tense |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. anakephalaiōsasthai means "to bring under one head/recapitulate." LOGOS's "unite" captures the outcome. PNEUMA adds "under the authority of" — the headship is implied in the verb (from kephalē, "head") but Paul does not say "authority" here. PNEUMA also shifts to future tense — the Greek is an aorist infinitive expressing God's purpose.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "authority" and shifts tense.

---

### v.13 — "sealed with the Holy Spirit of the promise" vs. "sealed with the promised Holy Spirit" vs. "he identified you as his own by giving you the Holy Spirit"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you were sealed with the Holy Spirit of the promise" | tō pneumati tēs epangelias tō hagiō — genitive "of the promise" |
| LOGOS | "were sealed with the promised Holy Spirit" | "Promised" — attributive rendering |
| PNEUMA | "he identified you as his own by giving you the Holy Spirit, whom he promised long ago" | "Identified...as his own"; adds "long ago" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Sealed with the promised Holy Spirit" is the standard rendering. PNEUMA interprets "sealed" as "identified you as his own" — one aspect of sealing, but sphragizō also implies authentication, protection, and ownership. PNEUMA also adds "long ago" and "by giving you."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA narrows "sealed" and adds content.

---

### v.14 — "pledge" vs. "guarantee" vs. "God's guarantee"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who is [the] pledge of our inheritance" | arrabōn — "earnest money/down payment" |
| LOGOS | "who is the guarantee of our inheritance" | "Guarantee" — functional equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "The Spirit is God's guarantee that he will give us the inheritance he promised and that he has purchased us to be his own people" | Full expansion; adds "he promised," "purchased us" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. arrabōn is a commercial term — a first installment guaranteeing the full payment. LOGOS's "guarantee" captures the function. PNEUMA expands into a full clause, adding "he promised" and "purchased us to be his own people" — content not in this verse.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA massively expands.

---

### v.17 — "a spirit of wisdom" vs. "the Spirit of wisdom" vs. "spiritual wisdom"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a spirit of wisdom and of revelation" | pneuma sophias — anarthrous (no article) |
| LOGOS | "the Spirit of wisdom and of revelation" | Capitalizes — identifies as Holy Spirit |
| PNEUMA | "spiritual wisdom and insight" | "Spiritual wisdom" — attribute, not person |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. pneuma sophias is ambiguous — "a spirit of wisdom" (attitude/disposition) or "the Spirit of wisdom" (Holy Spirit functioning as wisdom-giver). LOGOS capitalizes, identifying the Spirit as the agent. PNEUMA reduces to an attribute ("spiritual wisdom"), losing the personal dimension entirely. The ambiguity favors LOGOS's reading in context (the Spirit was just mentioned in vv.13-14).

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. pneuma sophias — Spirit as person vs. spirit as attitude. This affects whether Paul prays for a divine person to act or for a human disposition to develop.

---

### v.18 — "the eyes of your heart" vs. "your hearts will be flooded with light"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the eyes of your heart having been enlightened" | pephōtismenous tous ophthalmous tēs kardias — perfect passive participle |
| LOGOS | "having the eyes of your hearts enlightened" | Same — preserves metaphor |
| PNEUMA | "I pray that your hearts will be flooded with light" | "Flooded with light" — loses "eyes" metaphor |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul uses the vivid metaphor "the eyes of your heart" — an inner faculty of perception. PNEUMA changes this to "hearts flooded with light," which loses the "eyes" image and substitutes a different metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA substitutes a different metaphor.

---

### v.21 — "ruler and authority and power and lordship" vs. "rule and authority and power and dominion" vs. "ruler or authority or power or leader"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "every ruler and authority and power and lordship" | archēs, exousias, dynameōs, kyriotētos |
| LOGOS | "all rule and authority and power and dominion" | Abstract nouns |
| PNEUMA | "any ruler or authority or power or leader or anything else" | "Leader" for kyriotētos; adds "anything else" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The four terms denote categories of cosmic power — abstract "rule, authority, power, dominion" is the standard rendering. PNEUMA changes kyriotētos ("dominion/lordship") to "leader" (a person, not a category) and adds "or anything else" — Paul adds "every name that is named" separately.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA personalizes an abstract category and adds.

---

### v.22 — "gave him as head over all things to the assembly" vs. "gave him as head over all things to the church" vs. "made him head over all things for the benefit of the church"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "gave him [as] head over all things to the assembly" | edōken auton kephalēn — "gave him head" |
| LOGOS | "gave him as head over all things to the church" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "has made him head over all things for the benefit of the church" | "Made him head"; adds "for the benefit of" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek says God "gave" (edōken) Christ as head to the church — a gift. PNEUMA changes "gave" to "made" and adds "for the benefit of" — an interpretive addition that specifies the purpose.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA changes verb and adds purpose clause.

---

### v.23 — "the fullness of the one filling all things in all" vs. "the fullness of him who fills all in all" vs. "made full and complete by Christ, who fills all things everywhere with himself"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the fullness of the [one] filling all things in all" | to plērōma tou ta panta en pasin plēroumenou |
| LOGOS | "the fullness of him who fills all in all" | Same — concise |
| PNEUMA | "it is made full and complete by Christ, who fills all things everywhere with himself" | "Made full and complete"; adds "everywhere with himself" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek is compressed: the church is Christ's plērōma (fullness), and Christ fills all things in every way. PNEUMA expands to "made full and complete...everywhere with himself" — adding "everywhere" and "with himself" (not in Greek).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA expands the compressed christological formula.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 7 | "Followers," "authority," "long ago," "purchased us," "for the benefit of," "everywhere with himself" |
| PNEUMA structural changes | 3 | Restructures en agapē (v.4-5), substitutes metaphors (v.18), changes verbs (v.22) |
| en agapē placement | 1 | Modifies holiness (v.4) or predestination (v.5) |
| pneuma sophias ambiguity | 1 | Spirit as person vs. spirit as attitude (v.17) |
| Cosmic power terminology | 1 | Abstract categories vs. personalized leaders (v.21) |
| EMET transliteration | 2 | Ephesos, Christos |

**Overall pattern:** Ephesians 1 is one of the most theologically dense chapters in the NT, and the disagreements cluster around Paul's cosmic vocabulary. PNEUMA consistently expands compressed theological formulas into explanatory paraphrases — losing the density that is characteristic of Ephesians' style. The most significant disagreements concern the placement of "in love" (v.4-5), which determines whether love modifies believers or God, and the pneuma sophias ambiguity (v.17), which determines whether Paul prays for a divine person or a human disposition.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
